# Transportation Impact Brief — Jess (Anxious Pre-K Parent)

## Executive Summary

The school configuration vote has already happened — but the logistics that will greet your child at kindergarten entry have not been fully modeled. Under Option A, between 139 and 169 elementary families will be split across two buildings, and the district has confirmed it does not currently have enough bus drivers to execute any of the three configurations under review. Families entering the system in the next 1–3 years will inherit whatever structure is in place, with no opportunity to weigh in on the decision that shaped it.

---

## Key Finding

**After-care is already full at 4 of the 5 schools that would remain open under Option A.**

This is not a projection. It is the current enrollment state. Under Option A's 4-tier bell schedule, an estimated 118–144 new families would need before/after care they cannot currently access — at an annual cost to those families of **$143,640–$803,520**. Option B produces a care gap of $0.

For a family entering kindergarten in fall 2027 or 2028, this is the structural baseline they would walk into.

---

## Configuration Comparison

| Metric | Option A | Option B | Variant C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Families split across buildings | 139–169 (18–24%) | **0** | 123–150 (16–21%) |
| Bus drivers currently available | 20 confirmed | 20 confirmed | 20 confirmed |
| Drivers needed | 30 | 24 | 29 |
| **Driver shortfall** | **−10 to −13** | **−4 to −7** | **−9 to −12** |
| Bell schedule tiers | 3–4 | 3 | 3–4 |
| New families needing care | 118–144 | **0** | 104–128 |
| Family care cost (annual) | $143K–$804K | **$0** | $127K–$714K |
| Total fiscal exposure | $946K–$1.68M | $30K–$80K | $797K–$1.45M |
| As % of claimed savings | 43–112% | 1.4–5.3% | 36–97% |

*Source: SPEC-060 through SPEC-065. Driver count confirmed by Director of Operations Mike Natalie, 3/30/2026 board meeting.*

One figure cuts across all three options: **there is a universal driver shortfall.** Even the lowest-impact configuration (Option B) requires 4–7 drivers the district does not have. This was not modeled before the SEA staffing cuts — which eliminated 14% of bus driver positions — were approved.

---

## What's Missing

The district has not produced analysis on the following, all of which directly affect families entering the system:

- **PreK enrollment impact.** PreK participation is voluntary. No analysis has been done on whether split-building logistics or care gaps would suppress early enrollment among incoming families. (TC-006, TC-007 — parent testimony, not district modeling)
- **Care capacity expansion plan.** After-care is full at 4 of 5 schools. No plan has been presented for how capacity would be added to absorb 118–144 new families under Option A.
- **Driver hiring timeline and cost.** A shortfall of 4–13 drivers has been identified. No hiring plan, salary cost, or timeline has been published.
- **Family-borne cost disclosure.** The $143K–$804K annual care cost burden falls entirely on families. It does not appear in the district's budget savings claims.
- **Transport cost trajectory.** South Portland's per-pupil transport costs rose 50.2% from FY23 to FY25 — vs. a state average of 10.5%. No explanation or forecast has been published. (SPEC-065)

---

## Limitations

This analysis is built from publicly available budget documents, school board testimony through 3/30/2026, and structured modeling against those inputs. It cannot tell you:

- Which specific neighborhoods or feeder zones would produce split families under each option
- Whether care providers outside the district could absorb displaced families
- How the driver shortfall would be resolved — or at what cost — before fall implementation
- Whether PreK partnership program funding would be affected by configuration changes

The care gap cost range ($143K–$804K) is wide because it depends on assumptions about the share of split families who lack informal care options. The district has the enrollment and demographic data to narrow this range.

---

## Invitation to Improve

This brief would be materially sharper with the following district-held data:

1. **Current before/after care waitlist counts** by school — not just "full/open" status
2. **PreK enrollment by building and zone** — to model which incoming cohorts face split logistics longest
3. **SEA driver hiring plan** — positions, timeline, and cost to close the confirmed shortfall
4. **Care expansion plan or partnerships** — any agreements with providers to absorb displaced families
5. **Transport cost forecast** — district projection for FY26–FY27 given the 50.2% FY23–FY25 escalation

The district has stated that transportation logistics are "underway with a partner." Publishing that analysis before fall enrollment decisions would give incoming families the information they need to plan.
